title: When One Says Tiruchi, Is It Not a Turning-Point Conference? (1)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The printed source is authoritative for the numbered title series, conference dates, names and quoted material. OCR defects are corrected only in the derived Tamil reading copy; the canonical source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: It was while we celebrated the 92nd birthday of our DMK General Secretary, Inamana Perasiriyar, in North Chennai that I announced, amid the ocean-like gathering, that the party's State Conference would be held in Tiruchi on 15 and 16 February 2014. It took a long time for the applause that followed to subside.

The very next day after the Tiruchi State Conference was announced, Tiruchi district secretary Thambi K.N. Nehru arrived in Chennai. How did he get here so quickly? I need say nothing about Nehru. He never stands still; he is an outstanding activist, always moving at a run or a brisk walk. And when it is a State Conference, need one even ask? I announced it only on the 19th. Since then, he has already travelled to Chennai twice, to seek advice on selecting the venue and to decide whose name the conference pavilion should bear. Today's *Murasoli* itself reports that reception committee chairman Thambi K.N. Nehru convened a meeting at Kalaignar Arivalayam in Tiruchi with neighbouring district secretaries Thambis Periyannan Arasu, Nanniyur Rajendran, P. Duraisamy and S.S. Sivasankar, as well as Thambis A. Raja MP, N. Selvaraj, N. Siva, Tiruchi Selvendran, Anbil Periyasamy, K.N. Sekaran and city secretary Anbazhagan. After consulting them, he told reporters that the State Conference pavilion would be erected on 100 acres to accommodate approximately 1.5 million people.

From the birth of the DMK at Robinson Park in Chennai on 17 September 1949 until today, we have conducted nine State Conferences over these 64 years in a manner that astonished the world. The forthcoming Tiruchi gathering will be the tenth. Is that not a distinction of its own? Of these ten State Conferences, five have been in Tiruchi. That, too, is a distinction for Tiruchi. Ordinarily, the party president presides over a State Conference. In that capacity, I, this humble person, have presided over six of the ten, which is a distinction for me as well. Perarignar Anna presided over three and Navalar over one. Apart from these ten, two Special State Conferences were held under my leadership: at Salem on 27, 28 and 29 June 1997, and the DMK Golden Jubilee inaugural Special Conference at Tirunelveli on 17 and 18 September 1998.

To list the State Conferences: the first was held in Chennai on 13, 14, 15 and 16 December 1951 under Perarignar Anna; the second in Tiruchi on 17, 18, 19 and 20 May 1956 under Navalar Nedunchezhian; the third in Madurai on 13, 14, 15 and 16 July 1961 under Perarignar Anna; the fourth in Chennai on 29, 30 and 31 December 1966 and 1 January 1967 under Perarignar Anna; the fifth in Coimbatore on 25, 26, 27 and 28 December 1975 under my leadership; the sixth in Tiruchi on 9, 10 and 11 February 1990 under my leadership; the seventh in Coimbatore on 26, 27 and 28 March 1993 under my leadership; the eighth in Tiruchi on 26, 27 and 28 January 1996 under my leadership; and the ninth State Conference, combined with the Youth Wing Silver Jubilee Conference, in Tiruchi on 3, 4 and 5 March 2006 under my leadership. In addition, the Special State Conference was held at Salem on 27, 28 and 29 June 1997, and the DMK Golden Jubilee inaugural Special Conference at Tirunelveli on 17 and 18 September 1998, both under my leadership.

So far, 56 district conferences have also been held under the leadership of frontline DMK figures and good people who laboured every day for the party. On 19 occasions we have conducted conferences under such names as Special Election Conference, Anti-Hindi Conference, Regional Conference, Women's State Conference, Farmers' Wing State Conference, State Autonomy Conference, Dravidian Movement 75th Anniversary Platinum Jubilee Conference, Zonal Conference and Muthamizh Conference.

It is in this long historical succession of DMK conferences that the tenth State Conference will be held at Tiruchi on 15 and 16 February 2014. Since this conference comes before the parliamentary general election, it carries particular importance.

It was at a Tiruchi conference in Arignar Anna's time that the question was raised whether the DMK should contest elections. Anna told the assembled delegates: “Let the people's voting come later. First, DMK comrades at this conference, announce your decision on whether we should contest the election.” Ballot boxes were placed in the pavilion. Because most votes favoured “We may contest; we may participate in the election,” it was at that Tiruchi State Conference, conducted by Anna, that the democratic decision was taken that the DMK should contest elections.

In accordance with that Tiruchi decision, we now intend to hold our State Conference once again in Tiruchi before a parliamentary election. After Perarignar Anna passed away, we held the “Fifth Tiruchi District Conference” in this same Tiruchi in February 1970. It was in my concluding address there that I proclaimed the party's five great slogans:

1. We shall labour tirelessly in Anna's path! 2. We shall establish a society free of domination! 3. We shall forever oppose the imposition of Hindi! 4. We shall renounce violence and conquer poverty! 5. Autonomy for the State; federalism at the Centre!

Those five great slogans raised in Tiruchi on 22 February 1970 remain the DMK's anthem of the heart and continue to move us all. Tiruchi's brave son and our friend Anbil was reception committee chairman; the conference president was the sculptor of thought, elder brother C.P. Chitrarasu; and the inaugurator was our dear friend and party treasurer, Makkal Thilagam M.G.R. In *Murasoli*, Thambi Murasoli Maran painted a splendid word-picture of the procession at that district conference, which resembled a State Conference. I recall a small portion now because it is so apt:

“Anbilar has conducted a State Conference and falsely called it a district conference. The Tiruchi conference is significant in many ways. It is a notable event in the party's history after Anna. An American student researching our movement told me that no political party in the world uses speeches, drama, artistic performance, newspapers and posters as powerful propaganda weapons as the DMK does. We must add processions to that list.”
Murasoli Maran's article, which begins thus, gathers together the splendour of the many processions conducted by the party.

At the very start of that district conference procession, I narrowly escaped grave danger. The decorated chariot on which the leaders were seated stood behind the Anna statue at Chintamani in Tiruchi. We were sitting in it, while the head of the procession stretched as far as the eye could see.

Before it began, Anbil, Parangusam, M.S. Mani and Rabi asked me to garland the Anna statue there. I was not then as I am now! Today our treasurer Thambi M.K. Stalin runs wherever a party event calls him; I was just like that then, indeed even more so. The moment they mentioned a garland for Anna's statue, I leapt from the chariot and hurried towards it. Standing beside the statue, I placed a very large garland around Anna's neck.

It took only the blink of an eye. I did not know what had happened. Had I not snatched my hand away at once, I would have died that very day from an electric shock while garlanding Anna's statue, clasping it in an embrace. Yes, the statue had been decorated with electric lights for the conference. Very fine electric wires had been wound around the statue and its pedestal. The instant I placed the wet garland upon it, a violent shock struck me. As I was pulled towards it, I jerked my hand away and escaped. Anbil and the others stood speechless and anguished, realising in horror that something had happened. It lasted only a minute. Then I sat in the chariot and asked them to begin the procession. The sea of people carried me to the conference pavilion.

Was that really a district conference? Had people not gathered as though for a State Conference? Our dear friend M.G.R., then party treasurer and treasurer of the conference, delivered the inaugural address on the first day and left town the following morning for an urgent film shoot. The concluding address I gave as the conference's final event remains in my memory. I shall begin with it in my next letter.

It is in that Tiruchi that the State Conference will now be held, on 15 and 16 February. Before then, I shall continue to give you more details about it every Sunday. That is why I have begun to speak at some length in this letter. Do not think that there is still a month and a half before departure. Begin your travel arrangements now. Pay the advance for your vehicles. It is our custom to attend the conference with our families, is it not? Make suitable arrangements for that too. When a conference is held in Tiruchi, it is always a turning-point conference!
Affectionately,
M.K.
29 December 2013
